docs.title: AWS Industry Quest Labs 7–13: Manufacturing Mastery
Labs 7–10: Vehicle Telemetry & Manufacturing Context
docs.text:
Combined Duration: 2–2.5 hours

Concepts Covered: - OPC-UA connectivity patterns (industrial protocols) - Vehicle health metrics beyond sensors (firmware version, update status) - Manufacturing-specific KPIs (trucks on-time delivery, maintenance costs, fuel efficiency) - Historical data analysis for trend detection

Your Task (Real-Hardware Extension):

  1. Expand SiteWise Asset Model for your truck:
  2. Properties:
    • engine_temperature (from thermistor)
    • vibration_level (from GY-521)
    • cabin_lock_state (from RFID)

  3. Measurements (aggregated):

    • daily_uptime_percentage (manually calculated)
    • predictive_maintenance_days_remaining (computed)
    • anomaly_events_this_shift (count from rules)

  4. Update QuickSight Dashboard (from Week 14) with new visualizations:

  5. KPI Card: "Days Until Maintenance" (sourced from SiteWise)
  6. Time Series: "Vibration Trend 30-Day" (highlight anomalies in red)
  7. Map: Show truck location (simulated pit location)

  8. Table: Recent anomaly events with timestamp and severity

  9. Implement Cross-Device Rules:

  10. If any truck's vibration anomaly detected → trigger pit station alarm
  11. Aggregate metrics across fleet (in Week 18, imagine 20 trucks)

docs.title: Labs 11–13: Multi-Truck Simulation & Scaling
docs.text:
Duration: 1.5–2 hours

Concepts: - Scaling IoT workloads to fleet (1 → 20 trucks) - Simulating faults across multiple devices simultaneously - Cost optimization and data routing strategies - High-availability and failover patterns

Your Task:

  1. Scale TwinMaker Scene from 3 to 5 simulated trucks (in addition to your real truck-01):
  2. Truck-02: Normal operation
  3. Truck-03: Degraded (trending toward failure)
  4. Truck-04: Recent fault resolved, monitoring recovery

  5. Truck-05: Simulated failure scenario (stopped, needs rescue)

  6. Pit Station LCD Simulation (final software piece):

  7. Create a simple Node.js/Python web dashboard or HTML page showing:
    • Live truck status grid (6 cells: 1 real + 5 simulated)
    • Color coding: Green (OK), Yellow (Warning), Red (Critical), Gray (Offline)
    • Click truck → drill down to detailed metrics
    • Global KPIs: "Fleet Vibration Average", "Trucks in Maintenance Window"

Minimal Example (HTML):

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>RockCore Pit Station Dashboard</title>
    <style>
        .truck-card { 
            width: 150px; 
            padding: 20px; 
            margin: 10px;
            border-radius: 8px;
            text-align: center;
            font-weight: bold;
        }
        .truck-ok { background: #4CAF50; color: white; }
        .truck-warning { background: #FFC107; color: black; }
        .truck-critical { background: #F44336; color: white; }
        .truck-offline { background: #999; color: white; }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <h1>RockCore Mining – Pit Station Dashboard</h1>
    <div id="fleet-grid"></div>
    <script>
        const trucks = [
            { id: 'TC-01', status: 'ok', temp: 45, vib: 1.2, live: true },
            { id: 'TC-02', status: 'ok', temp: 48, vib: 1.5, live: false },
            { id: 'TC-03', status: 'warning', temp: 62, vib: 2.9, live: false },
            { id: 'TC-04', status: 'ok', temp: 42, vib: 0.8, live: false },
            { id: 'TC-05', status: 'critical', temp: 71, vib: 5.2, live: false }
        ];

        const grid = document.getElementById('fleet-grid');
        trucks.forEach(truck => {
            const statusClass = `truck-${truck.status}`;
            const badge = truck.live ? '🟢 LIVE' : '⚪ Simulated';
            grid.innerHTML += `
                <div class="truck-card ${statusClass}">
                    <div>${truck.id}</div>
                    <div>${badge}</div>
                    <div>Temp: ${truck.temp}°C</div>
                    <div>Vib: ${truck.vib}G</div>
                </div>
            `;
        });
    </script>
</body>
</html>
  1. Integrate Dashboard with Real Data:
  2. Pit station page fetches truck status from DynamoDB (populated by IoT Rules)
  3. Updates every 10 seconds

  4. Clicking a truck tile shows detailed metrics from SiteWise/QuickSight

  5. Demonstrate Fleet Scenario:

  6. Run your real truck with one anomaly
  7. Show pit station dashboard responding in real-time
  8. Show other trucks' simulated data updating
  9. Explain how this scales to 20+ trucks in production

docs.title: Troubleshooting & FAQs
docs.text:
Q: I didn't complete all 13 hours of Industry Quest labs. What should I do? A: Labs 1–6 are required — they cover TwinMaker modeling, data binding, and fleet simulation, which are all explicitly assessed in A6. Labs 7–13 are strongly recommended but do not have their own separate deliverable; they deepen the predictive maintenance understanding behind your CloudWatch alarm evidence. If time is short, complete Labs 1–6 in full first, then do as much of 7–13 as time allows.

Q: Can I submit Capstone without real truck hardware? A: No. A6 requires at least one real Pico W publishing live telemetry. You can simulate other trucks, but the "real" truck is mandatory.

Q: How much time should I spend on pit station web dashboard? A: Minimal viable: 30 minutes (basic HTML grid). Enhanced: 1–2 hours (add charts, drill-down, real-time WebSocket updates). Aim for MVP by end of Week 17.

Q: What if my TwinMaker scene has performance issues with 6 trucks? A: Start with 3 trucks (1 real + 2 simulated). That's sufficient for A6. Document the scaling plan in your architecture doc.

docs.text: Week 18 is for polishing your work, recording your final demo, and submitting the complete portfolio. This page guides you through the submission checklist and performance criteria mapping.

docs.title: Final Demo Video Requirements
docs.text:
Duration: 5-7 minutes (strict maximum)

Structure:

0:00-0:30 Introduction - Your name, student ID - "RockCore Mining Haul Truck Fleet Monitor - Assessment 6" - Quick overview of system components

0:30-2:00 Normal Operation Demo - Show truck with all sensors working - OLED displaying normal status - Pit station showing fleet summary - AWS QuickSight dashboard (or console if QuickSight not completed)

2:00-4:30 Fault Scenario Demonstrations (3 scenarios) - Scenario 1: Engine overheat (1 minute) - Trigger fault - Show truck response, pit station alert, SMS received - Scenario 2: Unauthorized access (45 seconds) - Show RFID rejection, security alert - Scenario 3: Vibration anomaly (45 seconds) - Show predictive maintenance alert

4:30-5:30 AWS Integration - Show Device Shadow in console - Show IoT Rule routing data - Show CloudWatch metrics or SiteWise dashboard

5:30-End Conclusion - Briefly mention how system maps to RockCore requirements - Thank you

Production Tips: - Use screen recording software (OBS, Zoom) for AWS console - Edit together truck footage + screen recording - Add timestamps in corner - Clear audio narration (use microphone if possible)

docs.text: The Capstone integrates all work from Weeks 1-14 into Assessment 6: Integrated Fleet System + AWS Dashboard. You'll create a complete RockCore Mining haul truck monitoring system with slave display node, demonstrate fault scenarios, and produce comprehensive documentation mapping to every performance criterion.

docs.title: Focus Areas
docs.text:
1. System Architecture

Create complete architecture diagram showing all components and data flows:

graph TB
    Truck[Pico W Truck HC-01<br/>All sensors from A1-A5] -->|MQTT/TLS| IoTCore[AWS IoT Core]
    IoTCore --> Shadow[Device Shadow<br/>Engine temp + Cabin lock]
    IoTCore -->|IoT Rule| SNS[SNS Alerts<br/>SMS/Email]
    IoTCore -->|IoT Rule| DynamoDB[(DynamoDB<br/>Historical data)]
    IoTCore --> SiteWise[IoT SiteWise<br/>Asset model]
    SiteWise --> QuickSight[QuickSight<br/>Dashboard]
    IoTCore -->|MQTT Subscribe| PitStation[Pico W Pit Station<br/>LCD display]
    CloudWatch[CloudWatch<br/>Metrics + Alarms] --> SNS

    Operator[Pit Operator] -->|Views| PitStation
    Operator -->|Views| QuickSight

2. Integration Checklist

  • Truck publishes all sensor data to single topic: rockcore/trucks/HC01/telemetry
  • Shadow updates for temperature and lock state
  • IoT Rule routes temp > 70°C to SNS
  • Pit station subscribes and displays truck status
  • CloudWatch alarm for vibration anomaly count
  • QuickSight dashboard shows live data
  • All code unified in single Arduino sketch

3. Unified Truck Code

Combine all assessments into one cohesive system:

// capstone_truck_complete.ino
// Integrates A1-A5 + AWS connectivity

#include <WiFiClientSecure.h>
#include <PubSubClient.h>
#include <ArduinoJson.h>
#include <DHT.h>
#include <MPU6050.h>
#include <MFRC522.h>
#include <Adafruit_SSD1306.h>
#include "secrets.h"

// All sensor pins from Weeks 1-9
#define DHTPIN 4
#define MQ2_PIN 34
#define FLAME_PIN 35
#define RAIN_PIN 36
#define LIGHT_PIN 39
// ... etc

// AWS topics
const char* TELEMETRY_TOPIC = "rockcore/trucks/HC01/telemetry";
const char* SHADOW_UPDATE = "$aws/things/rockcore-truck-HC01/shadow/update";

struct TruckState {
  float temperature;
  int gasLevel;
  float vibration;
  bool cabinLocked;
  int rainLevel;
  String status;  // "OPERATIONAL", "WARNING", "FAULT"
};

void publishTelemetry(TruckState state) {
  StaticJsonDocument<512> doc;
  doc["truck"] = "HC01";
  doc["temp"] = state.temperature;
  doc["gas"] = state.gasLevel;
  doc["vibe"] = state.vibration;
  doc["rain"] = state.rainLevel;
  doc["status"] = state.status;
  doc["timestamp"] = millis();

  char buffer[512];
  serializeJson(doc, buffer);
  client.publish(TELEMETRY_TOPIC, buffer);
}

void updateShadow(float temp, bool locked) {
  StaticJsonDocument<200> doc;
  JsonObject state = doc.createNestedObject("state");
  JsonObject reported = state.createNestedObject("reported");
  reported["temperature"] = temp;
  reported["cabin_locked"] = locked;

  char buffer[256];
  serializeJson(doc, buffer);
  client.publish(SHADOW_UPDATE, buffer);
}

void loop() {
  // Collect all sensor data
  TruckState state = collectAllSensors();

  // Publish to AWS
  publishTelemetry(state);
  updateShadow(state.temperature, state.cabinLocked);

  // Update local OLED
  displayOnOLED(state);

  // Check thresholds and trigger alerts
  checkFaultConditions(state);

  delay(5000);
}

4. Testing Requirements

Run complete system test documenting: - Normal operation (all green) - Temperature warning (60-80°C) - Temperature fault (>80°C) → SNS alert received - Vibration anomaly detection - Unauthorized RFID attempt - Rain detection triggering status change

docs.text:
Week: 16 Element: ICTIOT502 Device 2: Program second IoT device Phase: Capstone

Week 16 AWS Activity

The Industry Quest component for this week runs in parallel with this build. See the Week 16 AWS guide for the fleet simulation labs — those labs explain the multi-truck MQTT architecture your pit station implements.

docs.title: Overview for Assessment 6. It subscribes to MQTT topics and displays live fleet data on an LCD screen, giving pit operators real-time visibility into all truck statuses without accessing AWS console.
Hardware Requirements
docs.text:
• Pico W (second MCU)
  • 16×2 or 20×4 I²C LCD display
  • Piezo buzzer (for critical alerts)
  • RGB LED (fleet status indicator)
  • Push button (cycle through trucks) — expected unless you have a documented hardware constraint
  • Enclosure (3D print or project box)

Push Button Requirement

The push button enabling Mode 2 (Individual Truck Detail) is expected in your build. If you cannot implement it, you must document the specific hardware constraint in your portfolio. Omitting the button without documentation will be treated as incomplete functionality.

docs.title: Display Modes
docs.text:
Required vs optional modes

Mode 1 (Fleet Summary) and Mode 3 (Active Alerts) are required for Assessment 6. Mode 2 (Individual Truck Detail) is required if you implement button cycling — if your build includes the push button, all three modes must work. If you omit the button due to hardware constraints, document this decision in your portfolio.

Mode 1: Fleet Summary (required) 

RockCore Fleet
Trucks: 20  OK:18
WARN:1  FAULT:1
[HC-03 needs service]

Mode 2: Individual Truck Detail (required with button cycling) 

HC-01: OPERATIONAL
Temp:62C Vibe:1.2g
Gas:120  Rain:NO
Update: 3s ago

Mode 3: Active Alerts (required) 

!ALERTS!
HC-07: High temp 78C
HC-12: Vibration 2.4g
Press for details

docs.title: Alert Behavior
docs.text:
• Critical alerts → Red LED + continuous buzzer
  • Warnings → Yellow LED + intermittent beep
  • All OK → Green LED

docs.title: Pit Station Code
docs.text:
// pit_station.ino - Assessment 6 Device 2
#include <WiFiClientSecure.h>
#include <PubSubClient.h>
#include <ArduinoJson.h>
#include <LiquidCrystal_I2C.h>
#include "secrets.h"

LiquidCrystal_I2C lcd(0x27, 20, 4);  // 20x4 LCD

const char* FLEET_TOPIC = "rockcore/trucks/+/telemetry";
const char* ALERT_TOPIC = "rockcore/alerts/#";

#define BUTTON_PIN 4
#define BUZZER_PIN 26
#define RED_LED 25
#define GREEN_LED 27

struct TruckData {
  String id;
  float temp;
  float vibe;
  String status;
  unsigned long lastUpdate;
};

TruckData fleet[20];  // Support up to 20 trucks
int activeTrucks = 0;
int displayedTruck = 0;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  lcd.init();
  lcd.backlight();

  pinMode(BUTTON_PIN, INPUT);
  pinMode(BUZZER_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(RED_LED, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(GREEN_LED, OUTPUT);

  // Connect to WiFi and AWS
  connectAWS();

  // Subscribe to all truck telemetry
  client.subscribe(FLEET_TOPIC);
  client.subscribe(ALERT_TOPIC);

  lcd.setCursor(0, 0);
  lcd.print("RockCore Pit Station");
  lcd.setCursor(0, 1);
  lcd.print("Connecting...");
}

void mqttCallback(char* topic, byte* payload, unsigned int length) {
  StaticJsonDocument<512> doc;
  deserializeJson(doc, payload, length);

  // Parse truck ID from topic: rockcore/trucks/HC01/telemetry
  String topicStr = String(topic);
  int start = topicStr.indexOf("/trucks/") + 8;
  int end = topicStr.indexOf("/", start);
  String truckID = topicStr.substring(start, end);

  // Update fleet array
  updateFleetData(truckID, doc);

  // Refresh display
  displayFleetStatus();
}

void updateFleetData(String id, JsonDocument& doc) {
  // Find or create truck entry
  int index = -1;
  for (int i = 0; i < activeTrucks; i++) {
    if (fleet[i].id == id) {
      index = i;
      break;
    }
  }

  if (index == -1) {
    index = activeTrucks++;
    fleet[index].id = id;
  }

  fleet[index].temp = doc["temp"];
  fleet[index].vibe = doc["vibe"];
  fleet[index].status = doc["status"].as<String>();
  fleet[index].lastUpdate = millis();
}

void displayFleetStatus() {
  lcd.clear();

  // Count statuses
  int ok = 0, warn = 0, fault = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < activeTrucks; i++) {
    if (fleet[i].status == "OPERATIONAL") ok++;
    else if (fleet[i].status == "WARNING") warn++;
    else fault++;
  }

  // Display summary
  lcd.setCursor(0, 0);
  lcd.print("Fleet: ");
  lcd.print(activeTrucks);
  lcd.print(" trucks");

  lcd.setCursor(0, 1);
  lcd.print("OK:");
  lcd.print(ok);
  lcd.print(" WARN:");
  lcd.print(warn);
  lcd.print(" FAULT:");
  lcd.print(fault);

  // Show status LED
  if (fault > 0) {
    digitalWrite(RED_LED, HIGH);
    digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, LOW);
    tone(BUZZER, 1000, 500);
  } else if (warn > 0) {
    digitalWrite(RED_LED, HIGH);
    digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, HIGH);  // Yellow = R+G
  } else {
    digitalWrite(RED_LED, LOW);
    digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, HIGH);
  }
}

void displayTruckDetail(int index) {
  lcd.clear();
  lcd.setCursor(0, 0);
  lcd.print(fleet[index].id);
  lcd.print(": ");
  lcd.print(fleet[index].status);

  lcd.setCursor(0, 1);
  lcd.print("T:");
  lcd.print(fleet[index].temp, 1);
  lcd.print("C V:");
  lcd.print(fleet[index].vibe, 2);
  lcd.print("g");

  lcd.setCursor(0, 2);
  int secondsAgo = (millis() - fleet[index].lastUpdate) / 1000;
  lcd.print("Update: ");
  lcd.print(secondsAgo);
  lcd.print("s ago");
}

void loop() {
  if (!client.connected()) {
    reconnectAWS();
  }
  client.loop();

  // Button press cycles through truck details
  static bool lastButton = false;
  bool buttonNow = digitalRead(BUTTON_PIN);

  if (buttonNow && !lastButton) {
    displayedTruck = (displayedTruck + 1) % activeTrucks;
    displayTruckDetail(displayedTruck);
    delay(3000);  // Show detail for 3 seconds
    displayFleetStatus();  // Return to summary
  }
  lastButton = buttonNow;

  delay(100);
}

docs.title: Capstone: Testing Scenarios
docs.text:
Week: 17 Element: ICTIOT502 & ICTIOT503 - Test and rectify Phase: Capstone Testing

Week 17 AWS Activity

The Industry Quest component for this week runs in parallel with this testing. See the Week 17 AWS guide for Labs 7-13 — those labs cover predictive maintenance and multi-truck anomaly detection, which directly inform the CloudWatch alarm and SNS alert scenarios tested below.

docs.title: Overview of your complete system. You must simulate and document at least 3 real fault scenarios that demonstrate all assessments working together and triggering appropriate cloud alerts.
Scenario 1: Engine Overheat Emergency
docs.text:
Setup: - Truck operating normally (all green) - Temperature at 55°C

Trigger: - Heat thermistor above 80°C (apply warm object, warm air from heat gun/hair dryer, or simulate by holding between fingers)

Expected System Response: 1. Truck: Red LED illuminates, buzzer sounds 2. OLED: Displays "CRITICAL: Engine Fault" 3. AWS Shadow: Updates reported temperature to >80°C 4. IoT Rule: Triggers SNS alert → SMS/email sent 5. Pit Station: Shows HC-01 in FAULT state, red LED + buzzer 6. QuickSight: Dashboard shows temperature spike

Documentation: - Timestamp when fault triggered - Screenshot of SNS email/SMS - Pit station LCD photo showing fault - AWS console logs

docs.title: Scenario 2: Unauthorized Access Attempt
docs.text:
Setup: - Cabin locked (shadow shows cabin_locked = true) - RFID system active

Trigger: - Scan unauthorized RFID card

Expected System Response: 1. Truck: Red LED flashes, access denied tone 2. RFID logs: Unauthorized attempt recorded with timestamp 3. AWS Shadow: Does NOT update (cabin remains locked) 4. IoT Rule: Security alert published to rockcore/security/alerts 5. CloudWatch: Metric "UnauthorizedAccess" increments 6. Pit Station: Shows "Security Alert: HC-01"

Documentation: - System timestamp of attempt - AWS CloudWatch screenshot showing metric - RFID card ID logged - Shadow document showing cabin_locked still true

docs.title: Scenario 3: Predictive Maintenance - Vibration Anomaly
docs.text:
Setup: - Truck vibration baseline 1.2g (normal operation) - Anomaly threshold 2.2g

Trigger: - Shake ESP32 or attach to vibrating motor - Sustain elevated vibration for 30+ seconds

Expected System Response: 1. Truck: GY-521 detects anomaly, counter increments 2. OLED: Displays "WARNING: High vibration" 3. AWS Telemetry: Publishes vibration readings >2.2g 4. CloudWatch Alarm: "VibrationAnomaly" triggers after 3 consecutive samples 5. SNS: Sends maintenance alert "HC-01 needs tire inspection" 6. Pit Station: Shows HC-01 in WARNING state 7. QuickSight: Vibration trend chart shows spike

Documentation: - Time-series data showing vibration spike - CloudWatch alarm state change screenshot - Maintenance SMS received - Vibration anomaly count logged

docs.title: Scenario 4 (Bonus): Multi-Fault Cascade
docs.text:
Setup: - Simulate multiple simultaneous faults

Trigger: 1. Rain sensor detects water (60s) 2. Temperature rises to WARNING level 65°C (30s later) 3. Gas sensor detects leak (20s later)

Expected System Response: - System prioritizes faults (gas > temp > rain) - Multiple SNS alerts sent - Pit station shows highest priority fault first - QuickSight shows all three metrics spiking - Truck enters "SHUTDOWN" state after 3 critical faults

docs.title: Offline Resilience Test
docs.text:
Purpose: Demonstrate Device Shadow synchronization

Test Procedure: 1. Disconnect truck from WiFi 2. Change temperature from 55°C to 75°C (warm the thermistor to warning level) 3. Check AWS Shadow → shows last known (55°C) 4. Reconnect WiFi 5. Shadow automatically updates to 75°C 6. Timestamp shows when sync occurred

Documentation: - Shadow document before disconnect - Shadow document after reconnect - Serial monitor log showing reconnection - Sync latency measurement

docs.title: Latency Measurements
docs.text:
Truck to Cloud: - Measure time from sensor read to AWS IoT Core receipt - Target: < 500ms

Cloud to Display (Slave MCU): - Measure time from AWS publish to slave display LCD update - Target: < 1000ms total (sensor → cloud → slave display)

Method: 

// Add to truck code
unsigned long sensorTime = millis();
doc["sensor_timestamp"] = sensorTime;

// Pit station receives and calculates
unsigned long latency = millis() - doc["sensor_timestamp"];
Serial.print("Latency: ");
Serial.print(latency);
Serial.println("ms");

docs.title: Bandwidth Testing
docs.text:
• Count MQTT messages per minute
  • Calculate average payload size
  • Estimate bandwidth for 20-truck fleet

docs.title: Test Deliverables for Week 17
docs.text:
Test Report = Portfolio Section

The "Test Report" is not a separate submission document. It is the "Testing & Results" section of your portfolio (see Demo & Final Submission). Complete the content below and incorporate it directly into your portfolio document.

  1. Test Report (4-6 pages in your portfolio "Testing & Results" section):
  2. Executive summary
  3. All 3 scenarios documented with timestamps
  4. Screenshots/photos for each expected response

  5. Fault rectification log — use this format for each issue you encountered and resolved:

    Fault name Timestamp Symptoms observed Root cause Action taken Resolution confirmed Retest result e.g. Shadow not updating 10:34am Console shows stale state Wrong topic path in code Fixed topic string in secrets.h Shadow reflects live value ✅ Pass

  6. Test Video (5-7 minutes):

  7. Show normal operation
  8. Trigger each scenario
  9. Pan to show: truck response, slave display, AWS console, phone (SMS alert)

  10. Narrate what's happening

  11. Performance Data:

  12. Latency measurements table
  13. Bandwidth calculations

  14. Offline Resilience Test (required): disconnect truck from WiFi for 60 seconds, reconnect, and verify Device Shadow syncs reported state correctly. Document: disconnect timestamp, reconnect timestamp, Shadow update timestamp, and a screenshot of the Shadow console showing the sync.

  15. Updated Architecture Diagram:

  16. Add fault flow paths
  17. Show alert routing

docs.text:
Week 1-2 Structure

The combined Week 1-2 session (8 hours) covers Voltage, Current, and Ohm's Law fundamentals. Navigation references electronics/01.md. Note: A separate electronics/02.md file exists with LEDs & Breadboards content but is not yet integrated into the course structure—this should be consolidated with Week 3 (Advanced Output + Buttons) or clarified as a separate week depending on course redesign.

Schedule Note — Public Holiday & Equipment Changes

A public holiday fell between Sessions 4 and 5 (calendar Weeks 4–5), shifting the schedule by one week. Sessions 6 and 7 have been combined into a single Week 6/7 session (RFID, Tones & Environment) to align delivery with the current calendar position. The RTC (DS3231) has been removed; Assessment 2 now uses buzzer tones for access feedback instead.

Additionally, Session 4 and Assessment 1 have been updated: the DHT11 sensor is replaced by a thermistor, and the photocell (LDR) has been removed from A1 scope. The LDR is now covered in Session ⅚ as an additional task. See the Course Transition Log for full details.

docs.title: Session Introduction
docs.text: Welcome to the first week of the RockCore Mining IoT course! In this session, you’ll learn the basics of electronics—voltage, current, resistance, and Ohm’s Law—using the Tinkercad Circuits simulator (Official Guide). These fundamentals are essential for building reliable sensor systems for haul trucks. You’ll build a simple series circuit that measures current, and start assembling the truck chassis for hands-on sensor integration in later weeks.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

  • Identify voltage, current, and resistance in simple circuits
  • Apply Ohm’s Law to calculate values in a circuit
  • Use Tinkercad Circuits to simulate basic electronic components
  • Begin assembling the truck chassis for future sensor integration
  • Document your work for portfolio submission

docs.title: Session Overview
docs.text: This session introduces the core principles of electronics. You’ll use the Tinkercad simulator to experiment with voltage, current, and resistance, and start assembling the physical truck base. These skills are foundational for all future IoT device work in the course.

docs.title: Session Introduction
docs.text: This week you'll build the RGB status indicator for your RockCore Mining haul truck's front panel. Using Wokwi simulator, you'll learn LED control through digital output, PWM fading, and multi-color RGB combinations. This front panel will signal truck status (green=operational, red=fault, amber=warning) that your fleet management system can monitor locally and via cloud dashboards.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

  • Program digital output pins to control LEDs
  • Use PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) for LED brightness control
  • Control RGB LEDs to produce multiple colors
  • Debug LED circuits using serial monitor
  • Apply these skills to your truck's front panel status system

docs.title: RockCore Mining Context
docs.text:
Your haul truck needs a front panel RGB status indicator visible from 50 meters away for live fleet status:

  • Green: Truck operational, ready for loading
  • Amber: Warning state (e.g., engine temperature approaching threshold)
  • Red: Fault detected (stop loading immediately)

Today you'll build and test this system in Wokwi before implementing it on real hardware in Week 3.

docs.title: Task 1: LED Blink + Serial Debug
docs.text:
Build a circuit with a single LED that blinks every second and prints status to serial monitor.

Wokwi Starter: Create new Arduino Uno project, add LED + 220Ω resistor to pin 13.

// Week 2 Task 1 - LED Blink with Serial
void setup() {
  pinMode(13, OUTPUT);
  Serial.begin(9600);
  Serial.println("RockCore Truck Status: Initializing...");
}

void loop() {
  digitalWrite(13, HIGH);
  Serial.println("Status: ON");
  delay(1000);

  digitalWrite(13, LOW);
  Serial.println("Status: OFF");
  delay(1000);
}

docs.title: Task 2: PWM Fade for Warning State
docs.text:
Fade an LED smoothly to simulate the amber warning state.

// Week 2 Task 2 - PWM Fade (Amber Warning)
int brightness = 0;
int fadeAmount = 5;

void setup() {
  pinMode(9, OUTPUT);  // Must use PWM-capable pin
}

void loop() {
  analogWrite(9, brightness);
  brightness += fadeAmount;

  if (brightness <= 0 || brightness >= 255) {
    fadeAmount = -fadeAmount;  // Reverse direction
  }
  delay(30);
}

docs.title: Task 3: RGB LED Color Mixing
docs.text:
Control an RGB LED to produce green (operational), amber (warning), and red (fault) states.

Wokwi: Add RGB LED (common cathode) with three 220Ω resistors.

// Week 2 Task 3 - RGB Status Indicator
const int RED = 9;
const int GREEN = 10;
const int BLUE = 11;

void setup() {
  pinMode(RED, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(GREEN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(BLUE, OUTPUT);
}

void setColor(int r, int g, int b) {
  analogWrite(RED, r);
  analogWrite(GREEN, g);
  analogWrite(BLUE, b);
}

void loop() {
  // Green - Operational
  setColor(0, 255, 0);
  delay(2000);

  // Amber - Warning
  setColor(255, 191, 0);
  delay(2000);

  // Red - Fault
  setColor(255, 0, 0);
  delay(2000);
}

docs.title: Check Your Knowledge
docs.text:
Q1 – PWM vs Digital Output

Why can't you use digitalWrite() to fade an LED?

Answer

digitalWrite() only supports HIGH (5V) or LOW (0V). PWM using analogWrite() rapidly switches between HIGH and LOW to create intermediate voltages, controlling brightness.

Q2 – RGB Color Mixing

What RGB values would you use for amber/orange color?

Answer

Red=255, Green=191, Blue=0 (or similar ratios). Amber is red + partial green, with no blue component.

Q3 – Serial Debugging

Why is serial output useful when your LED circuit isn't working?

Answer

Serial output confirms your code is running and shows variable values, helping isolate whether the problem is code logic or hardware wiring.

docs.title: RockCore Mining Context
docs.text:
Pit station operators need to manually override truck status when performing maintenance. Today you'll add two buttons:

  • Green Button: Force operational status
  • Red Button: Emergency stop signal

Vibration from mining operations can cause "bouncing" – multiple false triggers from a single button press. You'll implement software debouncing to prevent false alarms.

docs.title: Task 1: Basic Button Input (ESP32)
docs.text:
Wokwi: Create ESP32 project, add button with 10kΩ pull-down resistor to GPIO 4.

// Week 3 Task 1 - Button Input
const int BUTTON_PIN = 4;
const int LED_PIN = 2;  // ESP32 onboard LED

void setup() {
  pinMode(BUTTON_PIN, INPUT);  // External pull-down resistor
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);
  Serial.begin(115200);
}

void loop() {
  int buttonState = digitalRead(BUTTON_PIN);

  if (buttonState == HIGH) {
    digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH);
    Serial.println("Button PRESSED");
  } else {
    digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);
    Serial.println("Button RELEASED");
  }
  delay(100);
}

docs.title: Task 2: Debounced Button
docs.text:
// Week 3 Task 2 - Debounced Button
const int BUTTON_PIN = 4;
int lastButtonState = LOW;
unsigned long lastDebounceTime = 0;
unsigned long debounceDelay = 50;  // 50ms debounce

void setup() {
  pinMode(BUTTON_PIN, INPUT);
  Serial.begin(115200);
}

void loop() {
  int reading = digitalRead(BUTTON_PIN);

  if (reading != lastButtonState) {
    lastDebounceTime = millis();
  }

  if ((millis() - lastDebounceTime) > debounceDelay) {
    if (reading == HIGH) {
      Serial.println("STABLE button press detected");
    }
  }

  lastButtonState = reading;
}

docs.title: Task 3: RGB Crossfade with Button Control
docs.text:
// Week 3 Task 3 - RGB Crossfade with Button Override
const int RED_PIN = 25;
const int GREEN_PIN = 26;
const int BLUE_PIN = 27;
const int OVERRIDE_BTN = 4;

int targetR = 0, targetG = 255, targetB = 0;  // Start green
int currentR = 0, currentG = 0, currentB = 0;

void setup() {
  pinMode(RED_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(GREEN_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(BLUE_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(OVERRIDE_BTN, INPUT);
}

void crossfade() {
  if (currentR < targetR) currentR++;
  if (currentR > targetR) currentR--;
  if (currentG < targetG) currentG++;
  if (currentG > targetG) currentG--;
  if (currentB < targetB) currentB++;
  if (currentB > targetB) currentB--;

  analogWrite(RED_PIN, currentR);
  analogWrite(GREEN_PIN, currentG);
  analogWrite(BLUE_PIN, currentB);
}

void loop() {
  if (digitalRead(OVERRIDE_BTN) == HIGH) {
    // Red fault state
    targetR = 255; targetG = 0; targetB = 0;
  } else {
    // Green operational state
    targetR = 0; targetG = 255; targetB = 0;
  }

  crossfade();
  delay(10);
}

docs.title: Check Your Knowledge
docs.text:
Q1 – Pull-down Resistors

Why do we need a 10kΩ resistor on the button input?

Answer

Without it, the GPIO pin "floats" when the button is not pressed, reading random values. The pull-down resistor ensures a stable LOW when the button is released.

Q2 – Debouncing

What causes button "bouncing" in industrial environments like mining?

Answer

Mechanical contacts bounce due to vibration, creating multiple HIGH/LOW transitions in milliseconds. Software debouncing ignores these rapid transitions.

Q3 – ESP32 vs Arduino Uno

What is one advantage of ESP32 for this truck project?

Answer

ESP32 has built-in WiFi/Bluetooth for AWS IoT connectivity (used in Weeks 10-14), more GPIO pins, and faster processing.

This week you transition from Wokwi simulation to real ESP32 hardware for the first time. You'll build the Engine Compartment Monitor using a thermistor (analog temperature sensor) and an RGB LED for colour-coded status display. This session completes Assessment 1, where you'll implement responsive colour logic tied to temperature thresholds – a critical skill for predictive maintenance and safety at RockCore Mining.

Course Update

The original Session 4 design used a DHT11 sensor and a photocell. Due to hardware reliability issues with the DHT11 kits, we have switched to a thermistor for temperature reading. The photocell component has been removed from A1 scope. See the Course Transition Log for details.

  • Read analog temperature from a thermistor using ESP32 ADC
  • Convert ADC values to temperature (°C) using the thermistor resistance formula
  • Control RGB LED brightness and colour using PWM
  • Map temperature readings to visual status indicators (colour = temperature state)
  • Complete Assessment 1 with fault rectification documentation

docs.title: RockCore Mining Context
docs.text:
Engine compartment fires are a leading cause of heavy equipment loss in mining. Your Engine Compartment Monitor must visually indicate the engine's thermal state via a colour-coded RGB LED. The system reads temperature from a thermistor and maps it to:

  • Green: Normal (< 60°C)
  • Yellow: Warning (60–80°C)
  • Red: Critical (> 80°C)

This design gives maintenance staff and operators an at-a-glance view of engine health without needing a screen or network connection.

docs.title: Thermistor (NTC 10kΩ)
docs.text:
• Type: Negative Temperature Coefficient — resistance decreases as temperature rises
  • Output: Analog voltage (read via ADC)
  • ADC range: 0–4095 (12-bit on ESP32)
  • Circuit: Voltage divider with 10kΩ fixed resistor
  • GPIO: 34 (ADC input, analog-only pin)
  • Accuracy: ±1–2°C with calibration, suitable for the 0–100°C monitoring range

docs.title: RGB LED (Common Cathode)
docs.text:
• Pins: R (GPIO 25), G (GPIO 26), B (GPIO 27) – all PWM-capable
  • Control: analogWrite(pin, 0–255) for brightness per channel
  • Resistors: 220Ω per channel (limit current)

docs.title: Wiring Diagram
docs.text:
ESP32 Pin Layout for Engine Compartment Monitor
================================================

Thermistor (NTC 10kΩ) with voltage divider:
  3.3V → [Thermistor] → GPIO 34 (ADC) → [10kΩ resistor] → GND

RGB LED (Common Cathode):
  Red cathode   → 220Ω resistor → GPIO 25
  Green cathode → 220Ω resistor → GPIO 26
  Blue cathode  → 220Ω resistor → GPIO 27
  Common anode  → 5V (or 3.3V with current limiting)

Breadboard Layout:
  Top rail: 5V (from USB or external PSU)
  Second rail: 3.3V (from ESP32)
  Bottom rail: GND

Components positioned:
  - Thermistor + 10kΩ resistor divider on left side
  - RGB LED on right side (resistors in series)

docs.title: System Logic Overview
docs.text:
The Engine Compartment Monitor performs these steps every 1000ms:

  1. Read thermistor ADC value from GPIO 34 (0–4095)
  2. Convert ADC to resistance using the voltage divider formula
  3. Convert resistance to temperature (°C) using the Steinhart–Hart equation
  4. Determine colour based on temperature:
  5. < 60°C → green
  6. 60–80°C → yellow
  7. ≥ 80°C → red
  8. Set RGB LED to the appropriate colour at full brightness
  9. Output debug info to serial (ADC value, temperature, colour state)

docs.title: Task 1: Thermistor Temperature Reading
docs.text:
Wokwi: Add a potentiometer to simulate the thermistor voltage divider output (0–3.3V on GPIO 34).

Objective: Read an analog voltage, convert to temperature, handle out-of-range values.

Pseudocode: 

PSEUDOCODE: Thermistor Temperature Reading
==========================================

CONSTANTS:
  BETA = 3950          // B-constant for NTC 10kΩ
  R_FIXED = 10000      // 10kΩ fixed resistor
  NOMINAL_R = 10000    // thermistor resistance at 25°C
  NOMINAL_T = 298.15   // 25°C in Kelvin

1. SETUP:
   - Configure GPIO 34 as analog input
   - Start serial communication (115200 baud)
   - Print welcome message

2. LOOP (every 1000ms):
   a. READ ADC value from GPIO 34
      - Result: 0–4095 (12-bit ADC on ESP32)
   b. GUARD against ADC extremes (0 or 4095 = wiring fault):
      - IF adcVal == 0 OR adcVal == 4095: print error, skip
   c. CALCULATE thermistor resistance:
      - resistance = R_FIXED × adcVal / (4095 – adcVal)
   d. CALCULATE temperature in Kelvin (Steinhart–Hart simplified):
      - tempK = 1 / (1/NOMINAL_T + (1/BETA) × ln(resistance / NOMINAL_R))
   e. CONVERT to Celsius: temp = tempK – 273.15
   f. PRINT ADC value and temperature to serial
   g. DELAY 1000ms

Implementation Hint (Sketch): 

#include <math.h>

const int  THERM_PIN  = 34;
const float BETA       = 3950.0;
const float R_FIXED    = 10000.0;
const float NOMINAL_R  = 10000.0;
const float NOMINAL_T  = 298.15;   // 25°C in Kelvin

float readThermistorTemp() {
  int adcVal = analogRead(THERM_PIN);

  // Guard against open/short circuit
  if (adcVal == 0 || adcVal == 4095) {
    Serial.println("ERROR: Thermistor read out of range");
    return -999;
  }

  float resistance = R_FIXED * adcVal / (4095.0 - adcVal);
  float tempK = 1.0 / (1.0 / NOMINAL_T + (1.0 / BETA) * log(resistance / NOMINAL_R));
  return tempK - 273.15;
}

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  Serial.println("Engine Monitor: Starting...");
}

void loop() {
  float temp = readThermistorTemp();
  if (temp != -999) {
    Serial.print("Temp: ");
    Serial.print(temp);
    Serial.println("°C");
  }
  delay(1000);
}

docs.title: Task 3: RGB LED PWM Control – Color & Brightness
docs.text:
Objective: Control RGB LED color and brightness using PWM.

Pseudocode: 

PSEUDOCODE: RGB LED PWM Control
================================

1. SETUP:
   - Configure GPIO 25, 26, 27 as PWM outputs
   - Set PWM frequency (default OK)

2. LOOP:
   a. SET LED to a color:
      - GREEN: R=0, G=255, B=0
      - YELLOW: R=255, G=255, B=0
      - RED: R=255, G=0, B=0
   b. ADJUST brightness (0–255):
      - multiply each RGB value by (brightness / 255)
   c. WRITE to GPIO pins using analogWrite()
   d. DELAY to see color change

Example: Fade through colors
   - For each brightness level 0→255:
     - Set color to RED
     - Write R=brightness, G=0, B=0
     - Delay 10ms

Implementation Hint: 

#define RGB_R 25
#define RGB_G 26
#define RGB_B 27

void setup() {
  pinMode(RGB_R, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(RGB_G, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(RGB_B, OUTPUT);
}

void setRGB(int red, int green, int blue) {
  analogWrite(RGB_R, red);
  analogWrite(RGB_G, green);
  analogWrite(RGB_B, blue);
}

void loop() {
  // Green at full brightness
  setRGB(0, 255, 0);
  delay(1000);

  // Yellow at full brightness
  setRGB(255, 255, 0);
  delay(1000);

  // Red at full brightness
  setRGB(255, 0, 0);
  delay(1000);

  // Red at half brightness
  setRGB(127, 0, 0);
  delay(1000);
}

docs.title: Task 4: Temperature → Colour Mapping
docs.text:
Objective: Read temperature from thermistor, map to RGB colour (green/yellow/red).

Pseudocode: 

PSEUDOCODE: Temperature Colour Determination
===========================================

1. READ temperature from thermistor (using readThermistorTemp())
   - Handle out-of-range errors (-999 return value)

2. DETERMINE colour based on temp:
   - IF temp < 60°C:
       colour = GREEN (R=0, G=255, B=0)
   - ELSE IF temp < 80°C:
       colour = YELLOW (R=255, G=255, B=0)
   - ELSE:
       colour = RED (R=255, G=0, B=0)

3. WRITE colour to RGB LED
   - setRGB(R_value, G_value, B_value)

4. PRINT temp and colour state to serial

Implementation Hint: 

void getColorFromTemp(float temp, int &r, int &g, int &b) {
  if (temp < 60) {
    r = 0; g = 255; b = 0;   // Green
  } else if (temp < 80) {
    r = 255; g = 255; b = 0; // Yellow
  } else {
    r = 255; g = 0; b = 0;   // Red
  }
}

// In loop:
float temp = readThermistorTemp();
if (temp != -999) {
  int r, g, b;
  getColorFromTemp(temp, r, g, b);
  setRGB(r, g, b);
  Serial.println(temp);
}

docs.title: System Flowcharts
docs.text: The following Mermaid diagrams illustrate the control logic for the Engine Compartment Monitor. Study these carefully before implementing code.

docs.title: Flowchart 1: Main Program Loop
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["START"] --> B["Initialize:<br/>Thermistor (GPIO 34)<br/>RGB pins 25,26,27"]
    B --> C["LOOP Start (1000ms)"]
    C --> D["Read Thermistor ADC<br/>GPIO 34 → 0–4095"]
    D --> E{ADC out<br/>of range?}
    E -->|Yes 0 or 4095| F["Print error<br/>skip this cycle"]
    E -->|No| G["Convert ADC<br/>→ Resistance<br/>→ Temperature °C"]
    F --> P
    G --> H["Determine Colour<br/>from Temperature"]
    H --> N["Write RGB values<br/>to GPIO 25,26,27<br/>analogWrite"]
    N --> O["Serial debug<br/>output"]
    O --> P["DELAY 1000ms"]
    P --> C

docs.title: Flowchart 2: Temperature State Determination
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["Read Temperature<br/>from Thermistor"] --> B{temp < 60°C?}
    B -->|YES| C["Colour = GREEN<br/>R=0, G=255, B=0<br/>Normal State"]
    B -->|NO| D{temp < 80°C?}
    D -->|YES| E["Colour = YELLOW<br/>R=255, G=255, B=0<br/>Warning State"]
    D -->|NO| F["Colour = RED<br/>R=255, G=0, B=0<br/>Critical State"]
    C --> G["Return (R,G,B)<br/>to main loop"]
    E --> G
    F --> G

docs.title: Check Your Knowledge
docs.text:
Q1 – Thermistor vs DHT11

Why is a thermistor a simpler choice than a DHT11 for analog temperature sensing in this application?

Answer

A thermistor is a passive resistive component — it requires no library, no digital protocol, and no timing-sensitive communication. It's read with a single analogRead() call via a voltage divider. DHT11 uses a proprietary one-wire digital protocol that can fail with timing issues, poor wiring, or library incompatibilities. For a simple threshold-based temperature monitor, the thermistor is more robust.

Q2 – PWM for RGB Control

Why use PWM (analogWrite) to control RGB LED brightness instead of reducing the 5V supply voltage?

Answer

PWM rapidly switches the LED on/off at a high frequency (typically 1000 Hz), and the human eye perceives it as continuous with perceived brightness proportional to duty cycle. Reducing supply voltage would reduce brightness but also reduce color intensity unpredictably. PWM is more precise, efficient, and allows independent control of each RGB channel.

Q3 – Temperature Thresholds in a Mining Context

Why are the warning thresholds set at 60°C and 80°C rather than, say, 40°C and 70°C?

Answer

Mining haul truck engines operate at high ambient temperatures — the engine bay can routinely reach 40–55°C under normal load. Setting warnings at 60°C and 80°C avoids false positives during normal operation while still providing early warning before reaching critical temperatures (typically 90–100°C for engine coolant). Thresholds should be calibrated to the specific engine model's operating specifications.

The Raspberry Pi Pico W used here has an OLED display and MPU6050 IMU soldered directly to its carrier board, providing a reliable I²C connection ideal for learning. docs.title: Session Introduction
docs.text:
This week you'll add the OLED display and accelerometer (GY-521/MPU6050) to your truck, plus a servo motor for simulating the dump bed. You'll learn I²C bus communication — a critical protocol for industrial sensor networks — on two different microcontrollers: the ESP32 (using C++ or MicroPython) and the Raspberry Pi Pico W (MicroPython only). Working across both platforms is a key mapping requirement for this unit of competency.

This session launches Assessment 3 (Tire & Suspension Health Monitor), which will be completed on the Raspberry Pi Pico W, and Assessment 4 (Payload & Load Management), which will be completed on a separate MCU using header pins on a solderless breadboard.

docs.title: RockCore Mining Context
docs.text:
Modern haul trucks use I²C sensor networks to monitor dozens of data points simultaneously. Today you'll implement:

  • OLED Display (Base panel): Shows truck status at a glance
  • GY-521 Accelerometer (Base panel): Detects tire imbalance and suspension issues
  • Servo Motor (Back panel): Simulates hydraulic dump bed for payload management

I²C allows all these devices to share just two wires (SDA/SCL) plus power – essential when space is limited.

docs.title: Task 1: OLED Display Setup (I²C)
docs.text:
Wiring: - SDA → GPIO 21 - SCL → GPIO 22 - VCC → 3.3V - GND → GND

// Week 5 Task 1 - OLED Display
#include <Wire.h>
#include <Adafruit_GFX.h>
#include <Adafruit_SSD1306.h>

#define SCREEN_WIDTH 128
#define SCREEN_HEIGHT 64
#define OLED_RESET -1

Adafruit_SSD1306 display(SCREEN_WIDTH, SCREEN_HEIGHT, &Wire, OLED_RESET);

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);

  if(!display.begin(SSD1306_SWITCHCAPVCC, 0x3C)) {
    Serial.println("OLED not found - check I2C address");
    while(1);
  }

  display.clearDisplay();
  display.setTextSize(1);
  display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE);
  display.setCursor(0,0);
  display.println("RockCore Mining");
  display.println("Truck ID: HC-01");
  display.println("Status: Ready");
  display.display();
}

void loop() {
  // Update display with sensor data (next tasks)
}

docs.title: Task 2: GY-521 Accelerometer (Vibration Detection)
docs.text:
Wiring: Shares same I²C bus as OLED (daisy-chain SDA/SCL).

// Week 5 Task 2 - GY-521 Accelerometer
#include <Wire.h>
#include <MPU6050.h>

MPU6050 mpu;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  Wire.begin();

  if (!mpu.begin(MPU6050_SCALE_2000DPS, MPU6050_RANGE_2G)) {
    Serial.println("MPU6050 not found - check wiring");
    while(1);
  }
}

void loop() {
  Vector normAccel = mpu.readNormalizeAccel();

  Serial.print("X: ");
  Serial.print(normAccel.XAxis);
  Serial.print(" | Y: ");
  Serial.print(normAccel.YAxis);
  Serial.print(" | Z: ");
  Serial.println(normAccel.ZAxis);

  // Vibration detection (for Assessment 3)
  float totalAccel = sqrt(normAccel.XAxis * normAccel.XAxis + 
                          normAccel.YAxis * normAccel.YAxis + 
                          normAccel.ZAxis * normAccel.ZAxis);

  if (totalAccel > 1.5) {  // Threshold tuning required
    Serial.println("WARNING: High vibration - tire imbalance?");
  }

  delay(100);
}

// Week 5 Task 3 - Servo Dump Bed Simulation
#include <ESP32Servo.h>

Servo dumpServo;
const int SERVO_PIN = 18;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  dumpServo.attach(SERVO_PIN);
  dumpServo.write(0);  // Start position: bed down
  Serial.println("Dump bed ready");
}

void loop() {
  // Simulate dump cycle
  Serial.println("Raising dump bed...");
  for(int pos = 0; pos <= 120; pos++) {
    dumpServo.write(pos);
    delay(15);
  }
  delay(2000);

  Serial.println("Lowering dump bed...");
  for(int pos = 120; pos >= 0; pos--) {
    dumpServo.write(pos);
    delay(15);
  }
  delay(5000);
}

docs.title: Raspberry Pi Pico W Track: I²C with Soldered Devices
docs.text:
Why a Second MCU?

The Raspberry Pi Pico W is introduced here because Assessment 3 (Tire & Suspension Health Monitor) must be completed on this board. The Pico W carrier board used in this course has the OLED display and MPU6050 IMU soldered directly to the PCB — no breadboard jumpers needed for I²C connections. This models industrial practice where permanent, soldered connections are essential for signal reliability.

Assessment 4 (Payload & Load Management) will be completed on a different MCU using a solderless breadboard with header pins. That contrast — reliable soldered I²C vs. breadboard I²C — is a key learning outcome for this unit of competency.

docs.title: Pico W Hardware Overview
docs.text:
Device Interface I²C Address Connection Type OLED (128×64 SSD1306) I²C 0x3C Soldered to carrier board MPU6050 IMU I²C 0x68 Soldered to carrier board

Both devices share a single I²C bus. Because they are soldered, the connections are permanent and low-resistance — meaning no intermittent "device not found" errors from a loose jumper wire.

docs.title: Step 1: Read the Pico W Pinout
docs.text:
Critical First Step

Before writing a single line of code, locate and download the official Raspberry Pi Pico W Pinout PDF. You need to identify:

  • Which GPIO pins are the I²C SDA and SCL lines (default: GP4 = SDA, GP5 = SCL on I2C0)
  • Which GPIO pins are used to supply power (VCC) to the OLED and MPU6050 on your specific carrier board

On this carrier board, the OLED and MPU6050 do not draw power directly from the 3.3V rail. Their VCC lines are wired to GPIO output pins — meaning you must set those pins HIGH in your code before the devices will power up and respond on the I²C bus.

Check Your Board

Look at your carrier board or ask your instructor for the schematic. Note down the pin numbers for:

  • OLED VCC pin → GP___
  • MPU6050 VCC pin → GP___

Write them in your portfolio. These are real-world skills — reading a schematic or pinout to understand how hardware is wired is a core competency in this unit.

docs.title: Step 2: Power the I²C Devices via GPIO
docs.text:
# Pico W - Step 2: Bring VCC pins HIGH to power I2C devices
# Replace VCC_OLED_PIN and VCC_IMU_PIN with the actual GPIO numbers
# from your carrier board pinout/schematic.

from machine import Pin
import time

VCC_OLED_PIN = 15   # CHECK YOUR BOARD — replace with correct pin
VCC_IMU_PIN  = 16   # CHECK YOUR BOARD — replace with correct pin

oled_pwr = Pin(VCC_OLED_PIN, Pin.OUT)
imu_pwr  = Pin(VCC_IMU_PIN,  Pin.OUT)

oled_pwr.value(1)   # HIGH = VCC on → OLED powered
imu_pwr.value(1)    # HIGH = VCC on → MPU6050 powered

time.sleep_ms(100)  # Allow devices to settle
print("I2C devices powered on")

What Happens If You Skip This?

If you forget to bring the VCC pins HIGH, the OLED and MPU6050 have no power. When you scan the I²C bus, you will see:

Found I2C devices: []

No devices at any address. This is exactly the same error you would see with a bad breadboard connection — and that is the teaching point. Whether it is a loose breadboard jumper or a GPIO pin left LOW, the result is identical: the device is unreachable on the bus.

docs.title: Pico W Task 1: I²C Scanner
docs.text:
Always run an I²C scanner first to confirm both devices are found before writing application code. This is good practice and saves debugging time.

# Pico W Task 1 - I2C Scanner
from machine import Pin, I2C
import time

# Power on I2C devices (update pin numbers from your board schematic)
VCC_OLED_PIN = 15
VCC_IMU_PIN  = 16
Pin(VCC_OLED_PIN, Pin.OUT).value(1)
Pin(VCC_IMU_PIN,  Pin.OUT).value(1)
time.sleep_ms(100)

# Initialise I2C bus (I2C0: SDA=GP4, SCL=GP5)
i2c = I2C(0, sda=Pin(4), scl=Pin(5), freq=400_000)

# Scan for devices
devices = i2c.scan()

if devices:
    print("Found I2C devices:")
    for addr in devices:
        print(f"  Address: {hex(addr)}")
else:
    print("No I2C devices found — check power pins and wiring!")

Expected output: 

Found I2C devices:
  Address: 0x3c   ← OLED
  Address: 0x68   ← MPU6050

If you see an empty list, revisit Step 2 and confirm your VCC pin numbers.

docs.title: Pico W Task 3: MPU6050 Accelerometer (MicroPython)
docs.text:
# Pico W Task 3 - MPU6050 Accelerometer
# Uses raw register reads — no external library needed

from machine import Pin, I2C
import struct
import time

# Power on devices
Pin(15, Pin.OUT).value(1)   # Update pin number for your board
Pin(16, Pin.OUT).value(1)
time.sleep_ms(100)

i2c = I2C(0, sda=Pin(4), scl=Pin(5), freq=400_000)

MPU_ADDR = 0x68

# Wake the MPU6050 (it starts in sleep mode)
i2c.writeto_mem(MPU_ADDR, 0x6B, b'\x00')
time.sleep_ms(100)

def read_accel():
    raw = i2c.readfrom_mem(MPU_ADDR, 0x3B, 6)
    ax, ay, az = struct.unpack(">hhh", raw)
    # Convert to g (±2g range, sensitivity = 16384 LSB/g)
    return ax / 16384.0, ay / 16384.0, az / 16384.0

while True:
    x, y, z = read_accel()
    print(f"X: {x:.3f}g  Y: {y:.3f}g  Z: {z:.3f}g")

    total = (x**2 + y**2 + z**2) ** 0.5
    if total > 1.5:
        print("WARNING: High vibration — tire imbalance?")

    time.sleep_ms(200)

docs.title: I²C Reliability: Solderless Breadboard vs Soldered Connections
docs.text:
Teaching Point — Why Devices 'Disappear'

One of the most common I²C debugging problems is a device that was working and then stops responding — or never responds at all. The error is always the same: no device found at that address.

Common causes on a solderless breadboard:

Cause Effect Loose jumper wire in breadboard hole Intermittent open circuit on SDA or SCL Two wires sharing a row incorrectly Short circuit between SDA and SCL Breadboard strip damaged or corroded High contact resistance, signal corruption VCC jumper not fully seated Device unpowered, no I²C response

On the Pico W carrier board, the OLED and MPU6050 are soldered — every joint is permanent and low-resistance. You will not see these intermittent failures.

For Assessment 4, you will wire an MCU to sensors on a solderless breadboard. Pay close attention to connection quality. If a device disappears from the I²C bus mid-session, check your breadboard connections first.

Debugging Workflow

  1. Run the I²C scanner — confirm all expected addresses appear
  2. If an address is missing: check power (VCC/GND), then SDA, then SCL
  3. Try a different breadboard row or a different jumper wire
  4. Reduce I²C frequency to 100_000 Hz if signals are marginal

docs.title: LDR Dimming — Ambient Light to LED Brightness
docs.text:
A photoresistor (LDR) outputs a varying analog voltage depending on light level. We can map this directly to the brightness of the RGB LED from Session 4 using PWM — the brighter the ambient light, the dimmer the status LED (simulating auto-brightness in the truck cabin).

Session 6/7

This LDR task is covered here in session ⅚. It will not appear in session 6/7 — consider it done once you complete the step below.

Wiring:

3.3V ──── [LDR] ──── GPIO 35 ──── [10 kΩ resistor] ──── GND

Connect the RGB LED red channel to GPIO 32 (or the pin used in session 4).

// Week 5/6 — LDR dimming the RGB LED (red channel)
const int LDR_PIN = 35;
const int LED_PIN = 32;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  ledcSetup(0, 5000, 8);       // PWM channel 0, 5 kHz, 8-bit resolution
  ledcAttachPin(LED_PIN, 0);
}

void loop() {
  int ldrValue = analogRead(LDR_PIN);   // 0 (dark) to 4095 (bright)

  // Invert: bright room → dim LED; dark room → bright LED (like auto-brightness)
  int brightness = map(ldrValue, 0, 4095, 255, 10);
  ledcWrite(0, brightness);

  Serial.printf("LDR: %d → LED brightness: %d\n", ldrValue, brightness);
  delay(200);
}

Cover the LDR with your finger (dark) and watch the LED brighten; uncover it (bright) and it dims.

docs.title: Check Your Knowledge
docs.text:
Q1 – I²C Addressing

How can multiple I²C devices share the same two wires without conflicts?

Answer

Each I²C device has a unique address (e.g., OLED=0x3C, MPU6050=0x68). The master (ESP32 or Pico W) specifies which address to communicate with, and only that device responds.

Q2 – Accelerometer Applications

Besides vibration, what else can the MPU6050 detect for your truck?

Answer

Tilt angle (is truck on steep incline?), sudden impacts (collision detection), and orientation (is truck tipped over?).

Q3 – Servo Power

Why might you need external power for the servo instead of ESP32's 5V pin?

Answer

Servos can draw 500mA+ under load. ESP32's 5V pin is limited to ~500mA total, which could brown out the microcontroller when the servo moves.

Q4 – GPIO Power Control on Pico W

On the Pico W carrier board, why are the OLED and MPU6050 VCC lines connected to GPIO output pins rather than the 3.3V rail?

Answer

Connecting VCC to a GPIO output pin lets the microcontroller power-cycle each device individually — useful for fault recovery and power management. It also means a device will not respond on the I²C bus if the GPIO pin is forgotten or left LOW, which is a deliberate learning exercise in this session.

Q5 – Breadboard Reliability

You're testing your Assessment 4 circuit and the MPU6050 was responding on address 0x68, but after moving the breadboard it no longer appears in the I²C scan. What is the most likely cause and how do you fix it?

Answer

A jumper wire has come loose from its breadboard hole, breaking the SDA or SCL (or VCC) connection. Re-seat all jumper wires firmly, especially those connected to the sensor. Run the I²C scanner again to confirm the device reappears.

docs.title: Session Introduction
docs.text:
This combined session covers the Operator Cabin Safety System and the Haul Road Proximity Monitor — two subsystems of the RockCore haul truck. You'll implement RFID card authentication with buzzer-based audio feedback, then add ultrasonic distance sensing to your roof panel for obstacle detection.

This session completes Assessment 2 (Operator Cabin Safety System).

docs.title: RockCore Mining Context
docs.text:
Only authorised operators may start a RockCore haul truck. Your cabin system must reject unauthorised cards with an audible alarm and approve authorised cards with a clear confirmation tone — workers in noisy mine sites rely on sound as much as sight.

Your roof panel must also detect nearby obstacles to protect the operator and the machine on hazardous haul roads.

docs.title: SPI vs I²C — Choosing the Right Protocol
docs.text:
Before wiring the RFID module, it's worth understanding why it uses SPI rather than I²C. You've already used I²C extensively (OLED, MPU6050) — here's how the two protocols compare:

Feature I²C SPI Wires 2 (SDA + SCL) 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS) Addressing 7-bit address per device Separate CS/SS pin per device Speed 100 kHz – 400 kHz typical 1–80 MHz typical Duplex Half-duplex Full-duplex Best for Multiple slow-to-medium devices (sensors, displays) Fast data transfer, one-to-one (RFID, SD cards, displays) Wiring complexity Low (shared bus) Higher (one SS pin per device)

Rule of thumb: - Use I²C when you have several devices to chain on two wires and speed is not critical - Use SPI when you need higher throughput or your device requires full-duplex communication (like the RFID-RC522)

In this course

You've used I²C for the OLED (0x3C) and MPU6050 (0x68) — both slow-to-medium sensors sharing two wires. The RFID-RC522 uses SPI because it exchanges data rapidly during card reads and has only one device on the bus.

docs.title: Task 1: RFID-RC522 Card Reading (SPI)
docs.text:
Wiring (SPI protocol):

RFID Pin ESP32 GPIO SDA (SS) GPIO 5 SCK GPIO 18 MOSI GPIO 23 MISO GPIO 19 RST GPIO 27 3.3V 3.3V GND GND 
// Week 6/7 Task 1 — RFID Card Reader (SPI)
#include <SPI.h>
#include <MFRC522.h>

#define SS_PIN  5
#define RST_PIN 27

MFRC522 rfid(SS_PIN, RST_PIN);

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  SPI.begin();
  rfid.PCD_Init();
  Serial.println("RockCore Access Control: Ready");
  Serial.println("Scan RFID card...");
}

void loop() {
  if (!rfid.PICC_IsNewCardPresent() || !rfid.PICC_ReadCardSerial()) {
    return;
  }

  String cardID = "";
  for (byte i = 0; i < rfid.uid.size; i++) {
    cardID += String(rfid.uid.uidByte[i], HEX);
  }

  Serial.print("Card UID: ");
  Serial.println(cardID);

  rfid.PICC_HaltA();
  delay(1000);
}

Why SPI here?

Notice we call SPI.begin() before rfid.PCD_Init(). The RFID module communicates entirely over the SPI bus — contrast this with the OLED and MPU6050 from session ⅚ that use Wire.begin() for I²C.

docs.title: Task 2: Buzzer Tones for Access Feedback
docs.text:
A piezo buzzer provides immediate audio feedback in a noisy mine environment. Design distinct tones that are easy to tell apart:

Wiring: - Buzzer → GPIO 26 - GND → GND

// Week 6/7 Task 2 — Buzzer Tones
const int BUZZER_PIN = 26;

// Access GRANTED — two rising notes (upbeat, positive)
void toneGranted() {
  tone(BUZZER_PIN, 880, 150);   // A5 — short mid note
  delay(200);
  tone(BUZZER_PIN, 1319, 300);  // E6 — longer high note
  delay(350);
}

// Access DENIED — three short descending buzzes (alarm-like)
void toneDenied() {
  for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
    tone(BUZZER_PIN, 400, 120);  // Low buzz
    delay(180);
  }
}

void setup() {
  pinMode(BUZZER_PIN, OUTPUT);
  Serial.begin(115200);
  Serial.println("Tone test — listen for granted / denied patterns");
}

void loop() {
  Serial.println("Playing GRANTED tone...");
  toneGranted();
  delay(2000);

  Serial.println("Playing DENIED tone...");
  toneDenied();
  delay(2000);
}

Tone design

Rising tones feel positive; falling or repeating low tones feel like a warning. Choose frequencies that cut through background noise (400–1400 Hz is easiest to hear over machinery).

docs.title: Task 3: RFID Access Control with Buzzer (Assessment 2)
docs.text:
Combine RFID reading with LED indicators and buzzer tones into the complete cabin access system.

// Week 6/7 Task 3 — Complete Cabin Access System
#include <SPI.h>
#include <MFRC522.h>

#define SS_PIN    5
#define RST_PIN   27
#define GREEN_LED 26   // swap with buzzer pin in your wiring if needed
#define RED_LED   25
#define BUZZER_PIN 33

MFRC522 rfid(SS_PIN, RST_PIN);

// Add your card UIDs here (scan with Task 1 to find them)
String authorisedCards[] = {"a3b2c1d0", "12345678"};
const int numAuthorised = 2;

void toneGranted() {
  tone(BUZZER_PIN, 880, 150);
  delay(200);
  tone(BUZZER_PIN, 1319, 300);
  delay(350);
}

void toneDenied() {
  for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
    tone(BUZZER_PIN, 400, 120);
    delay(180);
  }
}

bool isAuthorised(String cardID) {
  for (int i = 0; i < numAuthorised; i++) {
    if (cardID == authorisedCards[i]) return true;
  }
  return false;
}

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  SPI.begin();
  rfid.PCD_Init();
  pinMode(GREEN_LED, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(RED_LED, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(BUZZER_PIN, OUTPUT);
  Serial.println("=== RockCore Cabin Access System ===");
}

void loop() {
  if (!rfid.PICC_IsNewCardPresent() || !rfid.PICC_ReadCardSerial()) {
    return;
  }

  String cardID = "";
  for (byte i = 0; i < rfid.uid.size; i++) {
    cardID += String(rfid.uid.uidByte[i], HEX);
  }

  bool authorised = isAuthorised(cardID);

  Serial.print("Card: ");
  Serial.print(cardID);

  if (authorised) {
    Serial.println(" → ACCESS GRANTED");
    digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, HIGH);
    digitalWrite(RED_LED, LOW);
    toneGranted();
    delay(3000);
    digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, LOW);
  } else {
    Serial.println(" → ACCESS DENIED");
    digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, LOW);
    digitalWrite(RED_LED, HIGH);
    toneDenied();
    delay(3000);
    digitalWrite(RED_LED, LOW);
  }

  rfid.PICC_HaltA();
}

docs.title: Task 4: Ultrasonic Distance Sensor (HC-SR04)
docs.text:
Wiring:

HC-SR04 Pin ESP32 GPIO Trig GPIO 12 Echo GPIO 14 VCC 5V GND GND 
// Week 6/7 Task 4 — Ultrasonic Distance
const int TRIG_PIN = 12;
const int ECHO_PIN = 14;

float getDistance() {
  digitalWrite(TRIG_PIN, LOW);
  delayMicroseconds(2);
  digitalWrite(TRIG_PIN, HIGH);
  delayMicroseconds(10);
  digitalWrite(TRIG_PIN, LOW);

  long duration = pulseIn(ECHO_PIN, HIGH, 30000);  // 30 ms timeout
  return duration * 0.034 / 2.0;                   // cm
}

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  pinMode(TRIG_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(ECHO_PIN, INPUT);
}

void loop() {
  float dist = getDistance();

  Serial.print("Distance: ");
  Serial.print(dist);
  Serial.println(" cm");

  if (dist > 0 && dist < 50) {
    Serial.println("WARNING: Obstacle within 50 cm!");
  }

  delay(500);
}

docs.title: Task 5: Integrated Roof & Cabin System
docs.text:
Bring together the RFID access system and roof panel sensors into one program.

// Week 6/7 Task 5 — Full Cabin + Roof Panel Integration
#include <SPI.h>
#include <MFRC522.h>

#define SS_PIN     5
#define RST_PIN    27
#define GREEN_LED  26
#define RED_LED    25
#define BUZZER_PIN 33
#define TRIG_PIN   12
#define ECHO_PIN   14

MFRC522 rfid(SS_PIN, RST_PIN);

String authorisedCards[] = {"a3b2c1d0", "12345678"};
const int numAuthorised = 2;

void toneGranted() {
  tone(BUZZER_PIN, 880, 150);  delay(200);
  tone(BUZZER_PIN, 1319, 300); delay(350);
}

void toneDenied() {
  for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { tone(BUZZER_PIN, 400, 120); delay(180); }
}

bool isAuthorised(String id) {
  for (int i = 0; i < numAuthorised; i++) {
    if (id == authorisedCards[i]) return true;
  }
  return false;
}

float getDistance() {
  digitalWrite(TRIG_PIN, LOW);  delayMicroseconds(2);
  digitalWrite(TRIG_PIN, HIGH); delayMicroseconds(10);
  digitalWrite(TRIG_PIN, LOW);
  long dur = pulseIn(ECHO_PIN, HIGH, 30000);
  return dur * 0.034 / 2.0;
}

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  SPI.begin();
  rfid.PCD_Init();
  pinMode(GREEN_LED, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(RED_LED, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(BUZZER_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(TRIG_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(ECHO_PIN, INPUT);
  Serial.println("=== RockCore Cabin + Roof Panel Active ===");
}

void loop() {
  // --- Roof panel sensors ---
  float dist = getDistance();

  if (dist > 0 && dist < 50) {
    Serial.printf("ROOF: Obstacle at %.1f cm\n", dist);
    tone(BUZZER_PIN, 2000, 300);
  }

  // --- Cabin RFID ---
  if (rfid.PICC_IsNewCardPresent() && rfid.PICC_ReadCardSerial()) {
    String cardID = "";
    for (byte i = 0; i < rfid.uid.size; i++) {
      cardID += String(rfid.uid.uidByte[i], HEX);
    }

    if (isAuthorised(cardID)) {
      Serial.println("CABIN: Access GRANTED — " + cardID);
      digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, HIGH); digitalWrite(RED_LED, LOW);
      toneGranted();
      delay(3000);
      digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, LOW);
    } else {
      Serial.println("CABIN: Access DENIED — " + cardID);
      digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, LOW); digitalWrite(RED_LED, HIGH);
      toneDenied();
      delay(3000);
      digitalWrite(RED_LED, LOW);
    }

    rfid.PICC_HaltA();
  }

  delay(500);
}

docs.title: Check Your Knowledge
docs.text:
Q1 — SPI vs I²C

You need to add both an RFID reader and a second OLED to your ESP32. Which protocol does each use, and how does this affect wiring?

Answer

The RFID-RC522 uses SPI (requires its own SS/CS pin — GPIO 5). The OLED uses I²C (shares SDA/SCL with any other I²C device, identified by its 0x3C address). You'd wire both protocols simultaneously — SPI bus for RFID, I²C bus for OLED.

Q2 — Tone Design

Why not just use the same tone for both access granted and denied?

Answer

In a noisy mine environment, visual cues (LEDs) may be missed. Distinct tones provide an additional safety channel. Operators learn to associate the pattern with the outcome without needing to look at the device.

Q3 — RFID Authorisation

Your authorised card UIDs are stored in a plain array in code. What is the security risk, and how might you improve it?

Answer

Anyone with access to the source code can read the UIDs and add their own card. Improvements: store UIDs in encrypted EEPROM, use a backend server to validate, add a secondary PIN requirement.

// Week 8 Task 1 - Raw Vibration Data
#include <Wire.h>
#include <MPU6050.h>

MPU6050 mpu;

void setup() {

  Wire.begin();

  Serial.println("X,Y,Z,Magnitude");  // CSV header for plotting
}

void loop() {
  Vector accel = mpu.readNormalizeAccel();
  float magnitude = sqrt(accel.XAxis * accel.XAxis + 
                         accel.YAxis * accel.YAxis + 
                         accel.ZAxis * accel.ZAxis);

  // CSV format for data analysis
  Serial.print(accel.XAxis, 3);
  Serial.print(",");
  Serial.print(accel.YAxis, 3);
  Serial.print(",");

docs.title: Task 2: Moving Average Filter
docs.text:
// Week 8 Task 2 - Moving Average Filter
#include <Wire.h>
#include <MPU6050.h>

MPU6050 mpu;

const int WINDOW_SIZE = 10;
float xBuffer[WINDOW_SIZE];
float yBuffer[WINDOW_SIZE];
float zBuffer[WINDOW_SIZE];
int bufferIndex = 0;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  Wire.begin();
  mpu.begin(MPU6050_SCALE_2000DPS, MPU6050_RANGE_2G);

  // Initialize buffers
  for (int i = 0; i < WINDOW_SIZE; i++) {
    xBuffer[i] = 0;
    yBuffer[i] = 0;
    zBuffer[i] = 0;
  }

  Serial.println("Raw_Mag,Filtered_Mag");
}

float movingAverage(float buffer[], float newValue) {
  buffer[bufferIndex] = newValue;

  float sum = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < WINDOW_SIZE; i++) {
    sum += buffer[i];
  }

  return sum / WINDOW_SIZE;
}

void loop() {
  Vector accel = mpu.readNormalizeAccel();

  float rawMag = sqrt(accel.XAxis * accel.XAxis + 
                      accel.YAxis * accel.YAxis + 
                      accel.ZAxis * accel.ZAxis);

  float filteredX = movingAverage(xBuffer, accel.XAxis);
  float filteredY = movingAverage(yBuffer, accel.YAxis);
  float filteredZ = movingAverage(zBuffer, accel.ZAxis);

  float filteredMag = sqrt(filteredX * filteredX + 
                           filteredY * filteredY + 
                           filteredZ * filteredZ);

  Serial.print(rawMag, 3);
  Serial.print(",");
  Serial.println(filteredMag, 3);

  bufferIndex = (bufferIndex + 1) % WINDOW_SIZE;
  delay(50);
}

docs.title: Task 3: Anomaly Detection Algorithm
docs.text:
// Week 8 Task 3 - Vibration Anomaly Detection
#include <Wire.h>
#include <MPU6050.h>

MPU6050 mpu;

const int WINDOW_SIZE = 20;
float magBuffer[WINDOW_SIZE];
int bufferIndex = 0;
bool bufferFilled = false;

float BASELINE_THRESHOLD = 1.2;  // Normal vibration
float ANOMALY_THRESHOLD = 1.8;   // Abnormal vibration
const int ALERT_PIN = 25;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  Wire.begin();
  mpu.begin(MPU6050_SCALE_2000DPS, MPU6050_RANGE_2G);
  pinMode(ALERT_PIN, OUTPUT);

  Serial.println("=== RockCore Predictive Maintenance ===");
}

float calculateMagnitude() {
  Vector accel = mpu.readNormalizeAccel();
  return sqrt(accel.XAxis * accel.XAxis + 
              accel.YAxis * accel.YAxis + 
              accel.ZAxis * accel.ZAxis);
}

float getAverage() {
  float sum = 0;
  int count = bufferFilled ? WINDOW_SIZE : bufferIndex;

  for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) {
    sum += magBuffer[i];
  }

  return sum / count;
}

void loop() {
  float magnitude = calculateMagnitude();

  // Add to buffer
  magBuffer[bufferIndex] = magnitude;
  bufferIndex++;

  if (bufferIndex >= WINDOW_SIZE) {
    bufferFilled = true;
    bufferIndex = 0;
  }

  if (!bufferFilled) {
    Serial.println("Calibrating baseline...");
    delay(100);
    return;
  }

  float average = getAverage();

  Serial.print("Current: ");
  Serial.print(magnitude, 3);
  Serial.print(" | Average: ");
  Serial.print(average, 3);

  // Anomaly detection
  if (magnitude > ANOMALY_THRESHOLD) {
    digitalWrite(ALERT_PIN, HIGH);
    Serial.println(" | CRITICAL: Severe vibration - inspect tires/bearings");
  } else if (magnitude > BASELINE_THRESHOLD) {
    digitalWrite(ALERT_PIN, LOW);
    Serial.println(" | WARNING: Elevated vibration");
  } else {
    digitalWrite(ALERT_PIN, LOW);
    Serial.println(" | NORMAL");
  }

  delay(100);
}

docs.title: Task 4: Complete Predictive Maintenance System (Assessment 3)
docs.text:
// Week 8 Task 4 - Full Assessment 3 System
#include <Wire.h>
#include <MPU6050.h>
#include <Adafruit_SSD1306.h>

MPU6050 mpu;
Adafruit_SSD1306 display(128, 64, &Wire, -1);

const int WINDOW_SIZE = 20;
float magBuffer[WINDOW_SIZE];
int bufferIndex = 0;
bool bufferFilled = false;

float BASELINE = 1.2;
float WARNING = 1.5;
float CRITICAL = 1.8;

int anomalyCount = 0;
unsigned long lastAnomaly = 0;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  Wire.begin();
  mpu.begin(MPU6050_SCALE_2000DPS, MPU6050_RANGE_2G);
  display.begin(SSD1306_SWITCHCAPVCC, 0x3C);

  display.clearDisplay();
  display.setTextSize(1);
  display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE);
  display.setCursor(0,0);
  display.println("RockCore Truck");
  display.println("Vibration Monitor");
  display.println("Initializing...");
  display.display();

  Serial.println("=== Tire & Suspension Health Monitor ===");
}

float getMagnitude() {
  Vector accel = mpu.readNormalizeAccel();
  return sqrt(accel.XAxis * accel.XAxis + 
              accel.YAxis * accel.YAxis + 
              accel.ZAxis * accel.ZAxis);
}

String getStatus(float mag) {
  if (mag > CRITICAL) return "CRITICAL";
  if (mag > WARNING) return "WARNING";
  if (mag > BASELINE) return "ELEVATED";
  return "NORMAL";
}

void loop() {
  float mag = getMagnitude();

  magBuffer[bufferIndex] = mag;
  bufferIndex = (bufferIndex + 1) % WINDOW_SIZE;
  if (bufferIndex == 0) bufferFilled = true;

  if (!bufferFilled) return;

  // Calculate statistics
  float sum = 0;
  float maxVal = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < WINDOW_SIZE; i++) {
    sum += magBuffer[i];
    if (magBuffer[i] > maxVal) maxVal = magBuffer[i];
  }
  float avg = sum / WINDOW_SIZE;

  String status = getStatus(mag);

  // Track anomalies
  if (status == "CRITICAL" || status == "WARNING") {
    if (millis() - lastAnomaly > 5000) {  // New anomaly window
      anomalyCount++;
      lastAnomaly = millis();
    }
  }

  // Display update
  display.clearDisplay();
  display.setCursor(0,0);
  display.println("Vibration Monitor");
  display.println("----------------");
  display.print("Status: ");
  display.println(status);
  display.print("Current: ");
  display.println(mag, 2);
  display.print("Average: ");
  display.println(avg, 2);
  display.print("Anomalies: ");
  display.println(anomalyCount);
  display.display();

  // Serial logging
  Serial.printf("%lu,%.3f,%.3f,%s,%d\n", 
                millis(), mag, avg, status.c_str(), anomalyCount);

  delay(100);
}

docs.title: Check Your Knowledge
docs.text:
Q1 – Moving Average Trade-off

What's the downside of using a larger window size (e.g., 50 samples vs 10)?

Answer

Larger windows provide smoother data but increase lag – you'll detect anomalies slower. Critical for real-time safety systems where 1-second delay matters.

Q2 – Threshold Calibration

Why can't you use fixed thresholds (e.g., 1.8 g) for all trucks?

Answer

Different trucks, tire wear, load weights, and road conditions produce different baseline vibration. Each truck needs calibration period (20-50 samples) to establish its normal range.

Q3 – Predictive vs Reactive

How does this system provide predictive maintenance, not just alerts?

Answer

By tracking anomaly count over time, you can schedule maintenance when count reaches 5-10 (tire imbalance developing), not waiting for catastrophic failure.

docs.title: MCU Roles in This Session
docs.text:
Two Pico W Boards: Truck MCU + Display MCU

Session 9 requires 2 Pico W boards because the OLED display and GY-521 vibration sensor cannot run on the same board.

Architecture: - Board 1 (Master MCU): Runs all truck sensors (thermistor, RFID, servo, RGB, buzzer, GY-521). Publishes sensor data to AWS IoT Core and to Board 2 via inter-board communication. - Board 2 (Slave MCU): Runs the OLED display. Receives sensor data from Board 1 via I²C or serial communication and displays live truck status.

What this means practically:

  • Inter-board communication: Configure I²C (if boards are close) or serial (UART via USB bridge if separate). Master (Board 1) sends sensor readings; Slave (Board 2) displays them on the OLED.
  • The filtering algorithm you wrote in Session 8 runs on Master (Board 1). Slave (Board 2) is a pure display client.
  • Both boards run the Arduino framework (C++) with identical build toolchain.

Your truck has (on Board 1 — Truck MCU): - Front panel: RGB status indicator (Assessment 1 support) - Engine compartment: Thermistor (NTC 10kΩ) (Assessment 1) - Cabin (left panel): RFID (Assessment 2) - Base panel: GY-521 vibration (Assessment 3) + servo (Assessment 4) - Roof panel: Photoresistor, buzzer (Assessment 5)

Your display runs on (Board 2 — Slave MCU): - OLED (16×2 or 20×4): Shows live truck readings from Board 1 (Master)
