docs.title: Task 1: Board 1 Truck Controller - Sensor Aggregation
docs.text:
// Week 9 Task 1 - Board 1: Truck Controller
#include <Wire.h>
#include <MPU6050.h>
#include <Adafruit_SSD1306.h>

// Thermistor (NTC 10kΩ) — same circuit as Assessment 1
#define THERMISTOR_PIN 33
const float BETA    = 3950;
const float R_FIXED = 10000.0;

MPU6050 mpu;
Adafruit_SSD1306 display(128, 64, &Wire, -1);

struct TruckData {
  float temperature;
  int gasLevel;
  float vibration;
  int rainLevel;
  int lightLevel;
  float distance;
  bool accessGranted;
  String status;
};

TruckData truck;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  Wire.begin();
  mpu.begin(MPU6050_SCALE_2000DPS, MPU6050_RANGE_2G);
  display.begin(SSD1306_SWITCHCAPVCC, 0x3C);

  Serial.println("=== RockCore Fleet Truck HC-01 ===");
  truck.status = "INITIALIZING";
}

void updateSensors() {
  // Thermistor temperature read (Steinhart-Hart)
  int raw = analogRead(THERMISTOR_PIN);
  if (raw > 0 && raw < 4095) {
    float resistance = R_FIXED * raw / (4095.0 - raw);
    float tempK = 1.0 / (1.0 / 298.15 + (1.0 / BETA) * log(resistance / 10000.0));
    truck.temperature = tempK - 273.15;
  }

  Vector accel = mpu.readNormalizeAccel();
  truck.vibration = sqrt(accel.XAxis * accel.XAxis + 
                         accel.YAxis * accel.YAxis + 
                         accel.ZAxis * accel.ZAxis);

  truck.lightLevel = analogRead(36);

  // Update status based on all sensors
  if (truck.temperature > 80) {
    truck.status = "FAULT";
  } else if (truck.temperature > 60 || truck.vibration > 1.5) {
    truck.status = "WARNING";
  } else {
    truck.status = "OPERATIONAL";
  }
}

void updateDisplay() {
  display.clearDisplay();
  display.setCursor(0,0);
  display.setTextSize(1);
  display.print("Truck: HC-01");
  display.println(truck.status);
  display.println("----------------");
  display.print("Temp: ");
  display.print(truck.temperature, 1);
  display.println("C");
  display.print("Gas: ");
  display.println(truck.gasLevel);
  display.print("Vibe: ");
  display.println(truck.vibration, 2);
  display.print("Rain: ");
  display.println(truck.rainLevel < 2000 ? "YES" : "NO");
  display.display();
}

void loop() {
  updateSensors();
  updateDisplay();

  // Serial logging (JSON format for AWS later)
  Serial.print("{\"truck\":\"HC-01\",\"temp\":");
  Serial.print(truck.temperature);
  Serial.print(",\"gas\":");
  Serial.print(truck.gasLevel);
  Serial.print(",\"vibe\":");
  Serial.print(truck.vibration, 3);
  Serial.print(",\"status\":\"");
  Serial.print(truck.status);
  Serial.println("\"}");

  delay(2000);
}

docs.title: Task 2: Fault Injection Testing
docs.text:
Test Scenarios:

  1. Engine Overheat: Heat thermistor above 80°C (hold between fingers to warm, use heat gun carefully)
  2. Tire Imbalance: Shake base panel (GY-521)
  3. Unauthorized Access: Use unregistered RFID card
// Week 9 Task 2 - Fault Testing Code
// Add to master controller loop()

void testFaultConditions() {
  // Check for critical faults
  bool criticalFault = false;
  String faultMessage = "";

  if (truck.temperature <= -998) {  // readTemp() returns -999 on ADC out-of-range
    criticalFault = true;
    faultMessage = "SENSOR FAULT: Thermistor open circuit";
  }

  if (truck.temperature > 80) {
    criticalFault = true;
    faultMessage = "CRITICAL: Engine overheat";
  }

  if (truck.gasLevel > 500) {
    criticalFault = true;
    faultMessage = "CRITICAL: Gas leak detected";
  }

  if (truck.vibration > 2.0) {
    criticalFault = true;
    faultMessage = "CRITICAL: Severe vibration";
  }

  if (criticalFault) {
    digitalWrite(RED_LED, HIGH);
    tone(BUZZER, 2000, 1000);
    Serial.print("FAULT: ");
    Serial.println(faultMessage);
    truck.status = "SHUTDOWN";
  }
}

docs.title: Task 3: System Recovery & Graceful Degradation
docs.text:
// Week 9 Task 3 - Fault Recovery
void checkSensorHealth() {
  // Thermistor health check (ADC returns 0 or 4095 if open/short circuit)
  int raw = analogRead(THERMISTOR_PIN);
  if (raw == 0 || raw == 4095) {
    Serial.println("WARNING: Thermistor fault - using last known value");
    // Use cached value or safe default
  }

  // I²C device health check
  Wire.beginTransmission(0x3C);  // OLED
  if (Wire.endTransmission() != 0) {
    Serial.println("WARNING: OLED offline - continuing without display");
  }

  Wire.beginTransmission(0x68);  // GY-521
  if (Wire.endTransmission() != 0) {
    Serial.println("WARNING: GY-521 offline - vibration monitoring disabled");
    truck.vibration = 0;  // Safe default
  }
}

// Add to loop()
void loop() {
  checkSensorHealth();
  updateSensors();
  testFaultConditions();
  updateDisplay();
  delay(2000);
}

docs.title: Check Your Knowledge
docs.text:
Q1 – I²C Bus Load

Your system has 2 I²C devices (OLED on Board 2, GY-521 on Board 1). What's the maximum you can add?

Answer

I²C supports 127 devices (7-bit addressing), but practical limit is ~10-20 due to capacitance and speed degradation. Each device adds capacitance to SDA/SCL lines.

Q2 – System-Level Fault

If the master Pico W crashes, what happens to all subsystems?

Answer

Complete system failure – single point of failure. Industrial systems use redundant controllers or watchdog timers that reset the MCU on hang.

Q3 – Power Budget

Calculate total current draw: Pico W (100mA) + OLED (20mA) + 3 sensors (50mA each) + servo (500mA). Can you power this from USB (500mA)?

Answer

Total = 770mA. USB can't supply this safely. Need external 5V power supply (2A minimum) or separate servo power.

docs.title: What Changed — and Why
docs.text: This is a good moment to explain some updates we've made to the course materials.

docs.title: Equipment Issues
docs.text:
Early in the course we encountered two problems with our hardware kits:

  1. Microcontroller connection issues — PlatformIO had intermittent trouble connecting to the boards in class. We've worked through this and it is largely resolved; if you still have trouble, your instructor has a fallback workflow using the Arduino IDE.

  2. DHT11 sensor failures — The DHT11 temperature/humidity sensors in some kits proved unreliable, returning frequent errors or not working at all. Rather than troubleshoot faulty hardware, we've switched to a thermistor for Assessment 1.

docs.title: Assessment 1 Update — Thermistor Replaces DHT11
docs.text:
Your Assessment 1 Engine Compartment Monitor now uses a thermistor instead of a DHT11 sensor. The RGB LED requirements remain the same.

What's the same

  • RGB LED temperature colour display (green / yellow / red thresholds unchanged)
  • Temperature-to-colour mapping logic
  • Pseudocode, flowchart, and demo video deliverables
  • Fault rectification documentation
  • Due date: end of Week 4 (or as advised by your instructor)

What changed

  • DHT11 removed → replaced with thermistor (NTC 10kΩ)
  • Photocell (LDR) removed → no longer required in A1
  • No ambient light or auto-brightness logic required
  • Thermistor is read via ADC (analogRead) — no library needed

See Session 4 for the updated wiring and code guidance.

docs.title: Thermistor Quick Reference
docs.text: A thermistor is a resistor whose resistance changes with temperature. We use an NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) type — resistance decreases as temperature increases.

docs.title: Wiring (Voltage Divider)
docs.text:
3.3V ──── [Thermistor] ──── GPIO 34 ──── [10kΩ resistor] ──── GND
  • The junction between the thermistor and the fixed resistor connects to an ADC pin (e.g., GPIO 34)
  • No library required — just analogRead()

docs.title: Reading Temperature
docs.text:
const float BETA     = 3950;       // B-constant for NTC 10kΩ
const float R_FIXED  = 10000.0;    // 10kΩ fixed resistor

float readTemp(int adcPin) {
  int raw = analogRead(adcPin);
  if (raw == 0 || raw == 4095) return -999;  // out-of-range guard

  float resistance = R_FIXED * raw / (4095.0 - raw);
  float tempK = 1.0 / (1.0 / 298.15 + (1.0 / BETA) * log(resistance / 10000.0));
  return tempK - 273.15;
}

This gives a reading in °C. Apply the same colour thresholds as before:

Temperature LED Colour < 60°C Green 60 – 80°C Yellow ≥ 80°C Red

docs.title: Preparing for Session 5
docs.text:
Next week (calendar Week 6) we pick up with Session 5: I²C & Actuators.

Pre-session checklist

  • Complete any remaining Session 4 tasks (thermistor + RGB LED)
  • Ensure Assessment 1 submission is committed to GitHub
  • Read: PhysComp → I²C Protocol
  • Arduino IDE / ESP32 tasks only: Install Arduino libraries Adafruit SSD1306, Adafruit GFX
  • Pico W tasks (MicroPython): No Arduino library install needed — MicroPython libraries (ssd1306, imu) are imported directly in code. Instead, confirm Thonny IDE is installed and connects to your Pico W.
  • Locate OLED display and GY-521 accelerometer in your kit

Session 5 covers: OLED display, GY-521 accelerometer (MPU6050), and servo motor — all on the I²C bus. This session launches Assessments 3 and 4.

docs.title: AWS IoT Learning Plan: Foundations Overview
docs.text:
Official Course: AWS IoT Learning Plan: Foundations

Total Duration: 7 hours 50 minutes across 5 courses

Courses:

  1. Getting Started with AWS IoT – Introduction to AWS IoT, use cases, and benefits; key concepts and core services for industrial applications

  2. Securely Connecting AWS IoT Devices to the Cloud – Device-to-cloud authentication using X.509 certificates; secure MQTT communication and IoT policy configuration

  3. Handling AWS IoT Device Data and States – MQTT protocol, publish-subscribe model, IoT Rules Engine for processing telemetry, and Device Shadows for offline state management

  4. Managing AWS IoT Devices at Scale – Large-scale IoT architecture, fleet management, device indexing, and OTA updates for hundreds or thousands of devices

  5. Analyzing, Visualizing, and Gaining Insights from IoT Devices – Real-time analytics with QuickSight, Timestream, Grafana, and IoT Analytics for monitoring and insights

docs.title: Additional AWS Courses (Weeks 12–17)
docs.text:
OTA Updates in Edge Devices (NMTAFE Sandbox, 1 hour) – Link. AWS SimuLearn scenario: manage remote updates for IoT applications across industrial edge devices without manual intervention. Learn to deploy code changes, sensor updates, and firmware patches securely to hundreds of haul trucks in the field using AWS device management services.

ETL at the Edge (NMTAFE Sandbox, 1 hour) – Link. AWS SimuLearn scenario-based lab: solve real-world vehicle data collection challenges in areas with poor internet connectivity. Build ETL (extract, transform, load) solutions at the edge to manage data locally, ensure continuity, and synchronize with cloud when connectivity returns; directly applicable to haul trucks in remote mining areas.

AWS Industry Quest: Manufacturing and Automotive (NMTAFE Sandbox, 13 hours across Weeks 15–17) – Link. Game-based interactive learning covering 13 labs on digital twins, fleet management, predictive maintenance, and vehicle telemetry. Hands-on with AWS IoT TwinMaker, SiteWise, FleetWise, Greengrass, DynamoDB, QuickSight, and other AWS services. Directly applicable to haul truck capstone: build digital twins, manage fleet data, and implement real-world manufacturing solutions.

docs.title: How to Access AWS Skill Builder (Enrollment Process)
docs.text:
Step 1: Instructor Adds You to Sandbox Your instructor will add you to the NMTAFE AWS Academy Sandbox. You'll receive an email from Canvas LMS (not AWS) with activation instructions.

Step 2: Activate AWS Skill Builder Subscription via Canvas

  1. Log in to Canvas and open any AWS Academy course
  2. Select "Modules" menu
  3. Scroll to the last module "AWS T&C Resources"
  4. Review FAQs and click "Activate Your AWS Skill Builder Offer"
  5. Take the survey and consent to information sharing
  6. Click "Accept" to join the AWS Academy Skill Builder Program
  7. Note: Your email must be verified in Canvas to be eligible

Step 3: Wait for Activation (up to 3 days) After submission, AWS will process your request. You'll receive an email from AWS Skill Builder with login instructions.

Step 4: Access AWS Skill Builder

- Login using the email from AWS Skill Builder
- You'll automatically have access to the AWS IoT Learning Plan: Foundations courses
- You may also see additional AWS Cloud Quest courses (Cloud Practitioner, Serverless Developer, Solutions Architect)
